question_title: What will the seasonally adjusted unemployment rate be in Australia, for the following months? (February 2026)
summary: Australia's February 2026 unemployment rate should remain close to current levels. December 2025 was 4.1%, February 2025 was also 4.1%, and the RBA notes unemployment has been "broadly stable at around 4¼%" in recent quarters. While forecasts expect gradual rises to 4.4-4.5% by mid-2026, February is too early for significant drift. Month-to-month volatility is typically 0.0-0.2pp. Central estimate ~4.15%, with distribution slightly right-skewed reflecting expected gradual increase over the year.

factors.description: December 2025 unemployment at 4.1% provides strong anchor - most recent official data point
February 2025 was also 4.1%, providing year-over-year seasonal consistency
RBA and Treasury forecast gradual rise to 4.4-4.5% over 2026, suggesting upward drift
Month-to-month volatility typically 0.0-0.2pp, supporting narrow uncertainty bounds
RBA rate hike to 3.85% in Feb 2026 may modestly increase unemployment with lag
Employment growth slowed significantly in 2025 (165k vs 386k in 2024), suggesting underlying softening

resolution_criteria:
This question is a subquestion of a group question. This subquestion specifically pertains to (and resolves according to) the option 'February 2026'. The resolution criteria for the parent question is below.

This question resolves by the seasonally adjusted unemployment rate for each month, as released by the ABS in its [labour force statistics](https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/labour/employment-and-unemployment/labour-force-australia/), in percentages.
fine_print:
Each subquestion will resolve based on the first released final numbers (which are currently found on the monthly reports).

***

This question's information (resolution criteria, fine print, background info, etc) is synced with an [original identical question](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/22134) which opened on 2025-07-28 20:51:00. This question will resolve based on the resolution criteria and fine print contained above. However, if this question would resolve differently than the original question, then this question will be annulled. Additionally, if the original question's resolution could have been known before this question opened, then this question will be annulled.
